mnemonics.title: FIFO - First In First Out
mnemonics.content: FIFO: First In First Out - Just like a queue at a ticket counter. The first person who comes in gets served first, and the first page brought into memory leaves first when space is needed.

mnemonics.title: LRU - Least Recently Used
mnemonics.content: LRU: Least Recently Used - Remember 'Last Resort Used' - pages that haven't been used for the longest time are removed. Like removing the book from your shelf that you haven't read in the longest time.

mnemonics.title: Optimal - The Ideal Algorithm
mnemonics.content: OPT: Optimal Page Replacement - Remove the page that won't be used for the longest time in the future. Think of it as having a crystal ball to see which page won't be needed soon.

mnemonics.title: LFU - Least Frequently Used
mnemonics.content: LFU: Least Frequently Used - The page with the lowest hit count gets replaced. Like removing the least popular item from a menu.

mnemonics.title: Second Chance (Clock) Algorithm
mnemonics.content: Second Chance: Pages get a 'second chance' if recently used. If reference bit is 1, set to 0 and give another chance. Like giving someone a second opportunity if they've shown recent activity.

acronyms.usage: Use when understanding simple queue-based page replacement. The oldest page in memory is replaced first.
Use when you need to identify which page to replace based on recent access time. Check which page was used least recently.
Use as a theoretical benchmark. Not implementable in practice but used to compare other algorithms.
Use when page usage frequency matters. Replace page with lowest access count.
Use for classification-based replacement. Pages are classified based on Reference and Modify bits.
Contains information about each page including valid/invalid bit, frame number, and protection bits.
A cache for recently used page table entries to speed up virtual address translation.

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a straight line of people waiting. New people join at the back, and when someone leaves, they leave from the front. That's your frame queue in FIFO.
Picture a stack of plates in a cafeteria. When you use a plate, you move it to the top. When you need a new plate, you take from the top. The bottom plate is the Least Recently Used.
Imagine you have a future timeline showing all page accesses. Circle the page that appears furthest in the future - that's your victim.
Picture a circular clock face with pages as numbers. The hand moves around; if a page was recently used (bit=1), give it another chance and move on. If not used recently (bit=0), replace it.
Visualize a librarian looking for a book (page) that's not on the desk (memory). They must go to the shelves (secondary storage) to fetch it - that's a page fault.
Imagine more boxes causing more problems! Draw two frames vs three frames with FIFO and watch how more frames can actually increase page faults - counterintuitive but true.

analogies.analogy: Think of a bus where passengers (pages) board in order and the first to board gets off first when the bus is full. The oldest passenger leaves first.
Like a refrigerator where you keep foods you recently used at the front. When space runs out, you throw away the food item that hasn't been used in the longest time.
Like a professor who knows exactly which students will not attend future classes and removes those students from the class to make room for new ones.
Imagine a student constantly swapping between too many subjects because they can't fit them all in their study table, spending more time arranging books than actually studying.
Like a chef's cutting board - only the ingredients (pages) currently being used are kept close. The rest are stored in the pantry (secondary storage).
Like分配座位 in a classroom - each process gets a fixed number of seats (frames). Too few seats means students constantly stand up and sit down (page faults).
